narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like your undergraduate GPA of 3.70, which stands out well above the typical student's 3.21, and your LSAT score of 37, right in line with the average of 36.70. You're planning to attend part-time while managing a middle-income family background, which is less common since most students in the dataset were full-time from varied income levels. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 55.2% chance you'll face failure on that crucial test—enough to tip the scales toward rejecting your application to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, far below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model a notable dip in later academic performance compared to peers. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also well under the typical 5.7, added to that concern, though less strongly, suggesting early challenges that persisted. On a brighter note, your strong undergraduate GPA pushed firmly against failure, more than your LSAT did, highlighting how it reassured the model of your potential. Your middle family income and part-time status nudged slightly toward higher risk, as they're less common among successful bar takers, but these played minor roles. Overall, while your undergrad record shines brighter than most, the model weighed your lower high school deciles—especially in Year 3—as the dominant signals of bar exam risk, unlike typical admitted students who maintained stronger consistent rankings. This isn't a judgment on your resilience or drive as a Black woman breaking barriers; it's the model's data-driven lens. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your recent academic deciles, seek bar prep resources tailored for part-time students, or target schools with holistic reviews that value your unique background more deeply. You've already outperformed averages in key areas—building on that could rewrite this story. We're rooting for you.
